content.full_text:
November 16, 1976

Mr. Harry Simon  
2750 Bellflower Boulevard  
Long Beach, CA 90815

Re: Statement of Economic Interests of Renee Simon

Dear Mr. Simon:

This letter is in response to our telephone conversation of November 5, 1976, during which we discussed the obligations of your wife, who is a city councilwoman, to disclose the names of your law clients on her Statement of Economic Interests.

Your wife has disclosure obligations with respect to your law clients only if you own a ten percent or greater interest in your law firm (Gov. Code §82030). If you do have the requisite ownership interest, she is required to disclose the names of those clients who paid your firm a fee during the reporting period of which your pro rata share was $2,000 or more. The threshold figure is $2,000 (in effect, double the $1,000 mentioned in Gov. Code §87207(b)(2)) because your wife is required to disclose only her community property interest in your share of client fees.

In light of these reporting requirements, you ask what are your wife's responsibilities if you object to having your clients' identities disclosed. Under the Political Reform Act, a filer is required to use reasonable diligence in the completion of all statements and reports (Gov. Code §81004). Accordingly, if you inform your wife, or if your wife knows or has reason to believe who your clients are, your wife must disclose the required information even if you would prefer that she not do so. If, however, your wife does not know or have reason to believe who your clients are and if she asks but you refuse to provide her with this information, the Act does not require your wife to take additional action to determine the identity of the clients.

It is possible that your wife may know or have reason to believe who some or all of your clients are, but not know if any of them paid fees during the reporting period of which your pro rata share was $2,000 or more. If she asks and you refuse to tell her which of these clients paid the requisite fee, she is required to

Mr. Harry Simon  
November 16, 1976  
Page Two

report all their names, with the exception of those she actually knows or has reason to know paid fees too small to meet the reporting threshold.

Thus, by way of example, if your wife knows a client's name, but has no knowledge concerning the fee paid by the client, she must nonetheless disclose that client's identity. If, on the other hand, your wife either knows that the client did not pay a large enough fee or is aware of the type of services received by the client and can reasonably conclude that such services would not command a fee great enough to require disclosure, she would not be required to report the client's name.

I hope this information is of assistance to you. If you have any further questions, please call me.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Michael J. Baker  
Staff Counsel  
Legal Division

MJB:plh

sections.question_synthetic: What are the disclosure obligations for a city councilwoman regarding her spouse's law clients' names on the Statement of Economic Interests, and what actions must be taken if the spouse objects to disclosure?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A filer's spouse must disclose law clients' names if the spouse owns 10%+ of the law firm and clients paid fees with a pro rata share of $2,000+. The filer must use reasonable diligence; if the spouse knows a client's name and reasonable fee information, they must disclose it despite objections. If the spouse truly lacks knowledge and the spouse refuses to provide information, no additional action is required unless the spouse actually knows or can reasonably infer the fee threshold was met.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers present, but document is clearly an FPPC advice letter responding to a telephone inquiry. Conclusion information extracted from the substantive body paragraphs. Letter ends mid-sentence on page 2, but document appears complete based on signature block.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the disclosure obligations for a city councilwoman regarding her spouse's law clients' names on the Statement of Economic Interests, and what actions must be taken if the spouse objects to disclosure?

CONCLUSION: A filer's spouse must disclose law clients' names if the spouse owns 10%+ of the law firm and clients paid fees with a pro rata share of $2,000+. The filer must use reasonable diligence; if the spouse knows a client's name and reasonable fee information, they must disclose it despite objections. If the spouse truly lacks knowledge and the spouse refuses to provide information, no additional action is required unless the spouse actually knows or can reasonably infer the fee threshold was met.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to questions about Statement of Economic Interests disclosure obligations for a city councilwoman regarding her spouse's law clients, clarifying the 10% ownership threshold, $2,000 fee threshold (double the statutory amount to account for community property), and the scope of reasonable diligence required in disclosure.
